pmos

Engineering System Design — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. A product manager's (PM's) working mental model of how large software systems are built to serve millions of users without falling over. It starts from the one question every system-design choice answers — "what happens when there is more load than one machine can handle?" — and walks the toolkit an engineer reaches for: scaling, load balancing, caching, databases, and queues. Then it covers how those parts are kept running when hardware fails (redundancy), the trade-offs you cannot escape (the CAP theorem), and a worked example that ties the pieces together. Written for any PM. Every term is defined the first time it appears, and every acronym is spelled out on first use. The point is not to make you an architect — it is to let you follow an architecture discussion, ask the right question, and reason about cost, speed, and risk. Source provenance: curation-consensus, anchored on the open-source System Design Primer and the Wikipedia computer-science corpus. 18 verified sources.

What this is (one paragraph)

System design is the practice of deciding how the parts of a large software system fit together — which machines do what, where data lives, and how requests flow — so the whole thing stays fast and reliable as it grows. The single best free map of the territory is the System Design Primer, an open-source collection whose stated goal is to help engineers "Learn how to design large-scale systems" and "Prep for the system design interview." It is worth knowing that much of what a PM hears in an architecture review is also interview shorthand: the same vocabulary (load balancers, caches, shards, queues) shows up because it is the shared language of the field. This primer selects the handful of concepts that recur in almost every real system and frames how they relate, rather than teaching you to build any one of them.

Why this matters for a PM

You do not need to design systems, but the systems your team designs decide what your product can promise. Whether a feature can load in under a second, survive a traffic spike on launch day, or keep working when one data center goes dark — these are system-design outcomes, not feature decisions. When an engineer says "that will require sharding the database" or "we would need a queue for that," they are describing real cost and real risk, and a PM who can follow the reasoning negotiates scope better. The recurring theme below is that every gain (more speed, more scale, more uptime) is bought with added complexity or money. Understanding the trades lets you ask "what does that buy us, and what does it cost?" instead of nodding along.

Core concepts and definitions

Three ideas underlie everything else. Latency is the delay between asking for something and getting a response; as the engineering literature on latency notes, it "is physically a consequence of the limited velocity at which any physical interaction can propagate" — meaning distance itself costs time, which is why where your servers sit matters. Throughput is the companion idea: how many requests the system handles per second. The second key concept is state — information a server remembers between requests. A stateless server keeps no such memory, so any copy of it can handle any request; the reference on state in computer science explains why stateful systems "must maintain this history, creating dependencies that complicate horizontal scaling." That single distinction — stateless versus stateful — explains most of why some systems scale easily and others do not.

How a request actually flows

Picture what happens when someone opens your app. The request does not hit one computer; it travels through a chain. First it reaches a reverse proxy — a server that sits in front of the real servers and forwards traffic. The reference on the reverse proxy describes it as something that "appears to any client to be an ordinary web server, but in reality merely acts as an intermediary that forwards the client's requests to one or more ordinary web servers." (An API gateway — application programming interface gateway — is a reverse proxy specialized for routing app traffic.) From there the request may go to one of many identical application servers, which check a cache, talk to a database, and perhaps drop a task on a queue for later. Modern systems often split the application into microservices — many small, independently deployable services where "complex service assemblies are abstracted behind simple URI interfaces" — trading a simpler single codebase for the operational work of running many moving parts.

Scalability: scaling up vs. scaling out

Scalability is a system's ability to handle a growing amount of work, and there are exactly two ways to get it. The reference on scalability names them: "Scaling horizontally (out/in) means adding or removing nodes, such as adding a new computer to a distributed software application," versus scaling vertically, which means making one machine bigger (more processor, more memory). Vertical scaling (scaling up) is simpler — you buy a stronger machine and change nothing else — but it has a ceiling and a single big machine is a single thing to lose. Horizontal scaling (scaling out) adds more ordinary machines and has almost no ceiling, but it only works cleanly when servers are stateless, which is why the state distinction above matters so much. As a PM, the useful instinct is that "can we just scale this?" usually means scaling out, and scaling out is what makes every other concept here necessary.

Load balancing

Once you have many servers, something has to decide which one handles each request — that is a load balancer. The reference on load balancing describes the goal as "making their overall processing more efficient" and avoiding the situation where some machines are overloaded while others sit idle. A load balancer spreads incoming requests across the pool by simple rules (take turns, send to the least-busy server, or route by the user's location). It also quietly does health checking: if a server stops responding, the balancer stops sending it traffic, which is the first line of defense against a single failure becoming an outage. For a PM, the load balancer is the piece that makes "add more servers" actually translate into "handle more users" — without it, extra servers just sit there.

Caching and CDNs

The cheapest request is the one you never make. A cache stores copies of frequently used data somewhere fast, so repeat requests skip the slow original lookup. The reference on caching states the economics plainly: "the more requests that can be served from the cache, the faster the system performs." Caching is the highest-leverage speed tool in system design, but it introduces one hard problem — keeping the cached copy fresh when the real data changes (engineers only half-joke that cache invalidation is one of the genuinely hard problems). A content delivery network (CDN) takes caching geographic: it places copies of content at "edge" locations around the world to "provide high availability and performance ('speed') through geographical distribution relative to end users," as the reference on the content delivery network puts it. That is why a video or image loads fast no matter where the viewer is — they are pulling it from a nearby copy, not from your origin server.

Databases: SQL, NoSQL, sharding, replication

The database is the system of record — the organized store everything else trusts. The reference on the database frames the central modern fork as structure versus flexibility: relational SQL (Structured Query Language) databases enforce strict tables and relationships, while NoSQL ("not only SQL") databases trade some of that rigor for flexibility and "avoid join operations by storing denormalized data" (keeping related data together rather than split across tables). When one database machine cannot keep up, there are two scaling moves. Replication keeps copies of the data on several machines so that, per the reference on replication, "systems can continue operating when components fail (failover), serve requests from geographically distributed locations, and balance load across multiple machines." Sharding splits the data itself across machines — the reference on database sharding notes "each shard may be held on a separate database server instance in order to spread across multiple servers." Replication copies the whole; sharding divides it. Both add capacity and both add complexity (sharding in particular makes queries that span shards much harder), which is why "we need to shard" is engineer-speak for "this is now a real project."

Queues and asynchronous work

Not everything has to happen while the user waits. A message queue lets one part of the system hand a task to another and move on — the reference on the message queue explains that "messages placed onto the queue are stored until the recipient retrieves them." This is called asynchronous ("async") work: the user's action returns immediately while the slow part (sending email, processing a video, generating a report) happens in the background. Queues also absorb spikes — when traffic surges, work piles up in the queue instead of crashing the workers, and the workers drain it at their own pace. For a PM, the queue is the answer to "can the confirmation be instant even though the heavy processing takes thirty seconds?" Yes — you acknowledge fast and queue the rest. The trade is that background work is eventually done, not instantly done, so the product has to set the right expectation.

Redundancy and high availability

At scale, hardware failure is not a risk — it is a certainty, so systems are designed to survive it. The enemy to hunt for is the single point of failure: the reference defines it as "a part of a system that would stop the entire system from working if it were to fail," and the single point of failure is exactly what good design eliminates. The tool for eliminating it is redundancy — the reference on redundancy calls it "the intentional duplication of critical components or functions of a system with the goal of increasing reliability." Two load balancers, replicated databases, servers spread across data centers: each removes a single thing whose loss would take everything down. The goal these serve is high availability (HA), which the reference on high availability describes as "a characteristic of a system that aims to ensure an agreed level of operational performance, usually uptime, for a higher than normal period." Uptime is quoted in "nines" — 99.9% ("three nines") is about 8.8 hours of downtime a year; 99.99% is under an hour — and each extra nine costs disproportionately more redundancy.

The trade-offs you cannot escape

System design has a few genuine laws, and the most famous is the CAP theorem. CAP stands for Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance — and the reference on the CAP theorem states the catch: "if there is a network partition, one has to choose between consistency or availability." A network partition is when parts of the system temporarily cannot talk to each other; when that happens you must either refuse some requests to keep all data perfectly in sync (favoring consistency) or keep answering with possibly-stale data (favoring availability). Most large consumer systems lean toward availability through eventual consistency, which the reference on eventual consistency defines as the guarantee that "if no new updates are made to a given data item, eventually all read accesses to that item will return the last updated value." That is why you sometimes see a like-count or a comment lag by a few seconds — the system chose "always responsive" over "always identical everywhere." This is the deepest PM lesson here: contested requirements like "must be instant AND always perfectly consistent AND never down" can be physically impossible together, and naming which one to relax is a product decision.

A worked example: designing a URL shortener

Tie it together with a classic the System Design Primer uses for teaching — a service that turns a long link into a short one (think the link behind a "bit.ly"-style code) and redirects users who click it. The read path (someone clicks a short link) happens far more often than the write path (someone creates one), so the design optimizes reads. A user's click hits a load balancer, which routes to a stateless app server. That server first checks a cache for the short code, because popular links are requested constantly and serving them from memory is near-instant. On a cache miss it reads the database, which is replicated so reads spread across copies and a failure does not lose the mapping; once the link table grows past one machine it is sharded by the short code. Creating a link is the rare write, and any non-urgent follow-up work (analytics on who clicked) is dropped on a message queue so the user gets their short link instantly. Because a stale redirect is harmless for a moment, the system can favor availability and lean on eventual consistency — a concrete instance of the CAP choice. Every concept in this primer appears in that one small service, which is exactly why it is the canonical teaching example.

Concrete next actions you can take this week

You can build real fluency without writing any code. First, skim the table of contents of the System Design Primer — not to study it, but to see the shape of the field and recognize the vocabulary when it comes up. Second, pick one feature in your own product and trace its request path out loud with an engineer: where does it hit a cache, where does it read the database, is any work done on a queue? Third, ask your team one question: "where are our single points of failure?" — the answer maps directly to your product's real reliability promises via redundancy. Fourth, the next time a requirement sounds like "instant, consistent, and always-on at once," name the CAP trade explicitly and decide which property the product can relax. These four habits turn architecture reviews from something you sit through into something you steer.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • How databases work — the internals of the system of record: indexes, transactions, and the SQL-vs-NoSQL choice in depth.
  • How computers and the internet work — the layer beneath this one: what a server, a network request, and latency physically are.
  • Software engineering and the SDLC — how the systems described here are actually built, tested, and shipped over time.
  • DevOps — how large systems are deployed and kept running in production, where redundancy and high availability become daily operational practice.
  • APIs — the interfaces that let the services and microservices in these architectures talk to each other.

References

  1. github.com ·cited· (T1)

    The System Design Primer is the canonical free, open-source curation for this topic — its stated goal is to help engineers "Learn how to design large-scale systems" and "Prep for the system design interview," organizing the whole landscape into one map.

  2. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Scalability is a system's capacity to handle growing load, achieved two ways: "Scaling horizontally (out/in) means adding or removing nodes, such as adding a new computer to a distributed software application," versus scaling vertically by adding more power to one machine.

  3. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Latency is the delay between a request and its response, and it has a floor you cannot buy your way under: "Latency is physically a consequence of the limited velocity at which any physical interaction can propagate."

  4. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A stateless server keeps no memory of past requests, so any copy can handle any request — which is exactly why stateless designs scale out so easily, while stateful ones "must maintain this history, creating dependencies that complicate horizontal scaling."

  5. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Microservices split one big application into many small, independently deployable services — "Complex service assemblies are abstracted behind simple URI interfaces" — trading a simpler codebase for the operational complexity of running a distributed system.

  6. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A reverse proxy (the basis of an API gateway) is the front door that hides the servers behind it: it "appears to any client to be an ordinary web server, but in reality merely acts as an intermediary that forwards the client's requests to one or more ordinary web servers."

  7. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A load balancer spreads incoming requests across a pool of servers so none gets overwhelmed, "making their overall processing more efficient" and avoiding the case where some machines are overloaded while others sit idle.

  8. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A cache stores copies of hot data in faster storage so repeat requests skip the slow original lookup — and the economics are blunt: "the more requests that can be served from the cache, the faster the system performs."

  9. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A content delivery network (CDN) is caching moved geographically close to users, placing copies at edge locations to "provide high availability and performance (\"speed\") through geographical distribution relative to end users."

  10. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A database is the organized system of record managed by a database management system (DBMS); the central modern fork is structure versus flexibility — relational SQL databases enforce strict tables, while NoSQL databases "avoid join operations by storing denormalized data."

  11. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Sharding scales a database by splitting its rows across machines: "Each shard may be held on a separate database server instance in order to spread across multiple servers," which adds capacity at the cost of harder cross-shard queries.

  12. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Replication keeps multiple copies of data on different machines so that "systems can continue operating when components fail (failover), serve requests from geographically distributed locations, and balance load across multiple machines."

  13. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A message queue lets one part of a system hand work to another without waiting: "Messages placed onto the queue are stored until the recipient retrieves them," so a traffic spike fills the queue instead of crashing the worker.

  14. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Redundancy is the deliberate doubling-up that buys reliability: "the intentional duplication of critical components or functions of a system with the goal of increasing reliability," so one failed part does not stop the whole.

  15. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    A single point of failure is the thing you must hunt for and eliminate — "a part of a system that would stop the entire system from working if it were to fail" — and redundancy exists precisely to remove these.

  16. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    High availability is the goal redundancy serves, stated as a target: "a characteristic of a system that aims to ensure an agreed level of operational performance, usually uptime, for a higher than normal period," commonly quoted in "nines."

  17. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    The CAP theorem names the trade-off you cannot escape in a distributed system: "if there is a network partition, one has to choose between consistency or availability" — you cannot have both when the network splits.

  18. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Eventual consistency is the availability-favoring side of that trade-off: "if no new updates are made to a given data item, eventually all read accesses to that item will return the last updated value" — fast and always-on, but briefly out of date.

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_engineering-system-design.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
